1: wayyiśśāʾ yaʿăqōḇ ʿênāyw wayyarʾ wĕhinnēh ʿēśāw bāʾ — "Jacob looked up and saw — behold, Esau coming." The wĕhinnēh (behold!) signals a dramatic reversal: Jacob has arranged his household by descending order of love (v2 — servants first, Rachel/Joseph last), placing those he loves most at the rear, the position of greatest protection. The division reflects Jacob's triage under fear: the loved ones are shielded by the expendable. The narrative does not editorialize; the arrangement speaks for itself.
2: wayyāśem ʾet-hašĕpāḥôt wĕʾet-yaldêhen bāriʾšōnāh wĕʾet-lēʾāh wĕyaldêhā ʾaḥărōnîm wĕʾet-rāḥēl wĕʾet-yôsēp ʾaḥărōnîm — "He put the servants and their children first, Leah and her children next, and Rachel and Joseph last." The ordering mirrors the inverse of Jacob's emotional priorities (29:30 — he loved Rachel more than Leah). To place someone last in a procession before an armed encounter is to place them furthest from danger. The twice-repeated ʾaḥărōnîm (last) for Rachel and Joseph emphasizes their protected position. This arrangement will recur in v6-7 as each group approaches and bows.
3: wĕhûʾ ʿāḇar lipnêhem wayyištaḥû ʾarṣāh šeḇaʿ pĕʿāmîm ʿaḏ-giššetô ʾel-ʾāḥîw — "He passed ahead of them, bowing to the ground seven times until he came near his brother." Wayyištaḥû ʾarṣāh (bowing to the ground) — šāḥāh (H7812, to bow down, prostrate oneself) is both the posture of worship before deity and of submission before royalty. Seven full prostrations (šeḇaʿ pĕʿāmîm) is the protocol of a vassal approaching a suzerain, confirmed by the Amarna diplomatic letters (14th c. BCE): "I have bowed seven times, on the stomach and back." In Gen 27:29 the blessing Jacob stole included "nations bow down to you"; now Jacob bows to the very brother he supplanted. The seven prostrations are a complete reversal of the stolen blessing.
4: wayyāraṣ ʿēśāw liqrāʾtô wayyĕḥabbĕqēhû wayyippōl ʿal-ṣawwārô wayyiššāqēhû wayyiḇkû — "Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck, and kissed him. And they wept." The four verbs — rûṣ (ran), ḥābaq (embraced), nāšaq (kissed), bāḵāh (wept) — form the only tetracolon of reunion in Genesis. The identical three-verb cluster (rûṣ/ḥābaq/nāšaq) appeared at Laban's greeting of Jacob in 29:13, but there the tears are absent; Esau's tears mark the emotion as genuine rather than political. Luke 15:20's description of the father who "ran and fell on his neck and kissed" his returning son uses the same gesture-vocabulary, suggesting the Esau-Jacob reunion is the narrative archetype Jesus draws on for the prodigal parable.
5: wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-ʿênāyw wayyarʾ ʾet-hannāšîm wĕʾet-hayyĕlāḏîm wayyōʾmer mî-ʾēlleh lāḵ wayyōʾmer hayyĕlāḏîm ʾăšer-ḥānān ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ʿaḇdĕḵā — "Jacob said, 'The children God has graciously given your servant.'" The verb ḥānan (H2603, to be gracious, to show favor, to give as a gift of grace) — the root of the name chen (grace, favor) — is Jacob's theological attribution: these children are not an achievement of his own striving but a gift of divine grace. After twenty years in which Jacob's household was built through rivalry, deception, and labor, he presents his family to Esau with the vocabulary of undeserved gift. The address ʿaḇdĕḵā (your servant) continues the diplomatic humility of v3.
6: waṯṯiggašnāh hašĕpāḥôt hēnnāh wĕyaldêhen waṯṯišṯaḥăwênāh — "The female servants and their children came forward and bowed down." The approach sequence mirrors the procession order of v2 — servants first. The Hithpael of šāḥāh (waṯṯišṯaḥăwênāh, they prostrated themselves) is the same bow Jacob performed in v3. The entire household enacts the diplomatic submission Jacob modeled. The narrative records the bowing without dialogue; the servants have no named speech-act here.
7: waṯṯiggaš gam-lēʾāh wĕyaldêhā wayyišṯaḥăwû wĕʾaḥar niggaš yôsēp wĕrāḥēl wayyišṯaḥăwû — "Then Leah and her children came and bowed down. Last of all came Joseph and Rachel, and they bowed down." The sequential introduction — servants, then Leah's group, then Joseph and Rachel — is the reverse of social standing (Rachel is the beloved) and the reverse of birth order (Reuben was born first). The narrator preserves the emotional order of protection that Jacob established in vv1-2. Joseph, the last-born and most beloved (his name means "may God add"), is introduced last — a placement that will frame his role in the subsequent Joseph narrative.
8: maḥăneh hazzeh ʾăšer pāgāštî — "What is the meaning of all this camp I met?" The word maḥăneh (H4264, camp, company) — the same word Jacob used for his own camp in 32:2 (Mahanaim) and for Esau's approaching camp (32:8). Esau's inquiry prompts Jacob's language of gift in v8b: limṣōʾ ḥēn bĕʿênê ʾăḏōnî — "to find favor (ḥēn) in the eyes of my lord." The gift (minḥāh, H4503) is a cultic/tribute term — the same word for the cereal offering brought before deity in the Levitical system. Jacob has selected the tribute-vocabulary deliberately.
9: wayyōʾmer ʿēśāw yeš-lî rāḇ ʾāḥî yĕhî-lĕḵā ʾăšer-lāḵ — "Esau said, 'I have plenty, my brother. Keep what is yours.'" Yeš-lî rāḇ — "there is to me much/plenty." The word rāḇ (H7227, much, many, great) is Esau's self-assessment. Jacob will counter in v11 with yeš-lî ḵōl — "I have everything/all." The contrast is theologically significant: Esau has rāḇ (much) — abundance by human measure; Jacob has ḵōl (all) — a covenantal totality. The address ʾāḥî (my brother) — the first time Esau uses this word in the reconciliation — signals that he is ending the hostility of ch 27.
10: rāʾîtî pāneyḵā kirʾōt pĕnê ʾĕlōhîm waṯṯirṣēnî — "To see your face is like seeing the face of God, now that you have received me favorably." The comparison kirʾōt pĕnê ʾĕlōhîm (like seeing the face of God) is a direct echo of 32:30, where Jacob named the Peniel site penî-ʾēl because "I have seen God face to face and my life was preserved." To see a powerful figure whose acceptance you feared is existentially analogous to the theophanic encounter. The minḥāh Jacob presses on Esau functions as a tribute-offering brought before a potentially wrathful lord — the cultic register is intentional. Waṯṯirṣēnî (you have accepted me favorably, Qal imperfect of rāṣāh, H7521) — the same verb used for divine acceptance of offerings in Lev 1:4; 22:21.
11: qah-nāʾ ʾet-birḵātî ʾăšer hūḇāʾt lāḵ kî-ḥannannî ʾĕlōhîm wĕḵî yeš-lî-ḵōl — "Please accept my blessing (birḵātî) which is brought to you." Birḵāh (H1293, blessing) — the gift of animals is called Jacob's birḵāh. The word choice is striking: in 27:35 Isaac said to Esau "your brother came with deceit and took your blessing (birḵāteḵā)." Now Jacob names his tribute to Esau as birḵātî (my blessing). Whether Jacob is consciously returning what was stolen is not stated, but the narrative use of the same noun creates an interpretive frame. ḵî ḥannannî ʾĕlōhîm — "because God has been gracious to me" — the second use of ḥānan in this chapter (cf. v5), marking grace as the theological signature of Jacob's household narrative.
12: wayyōʾmer nisĕʿāh wĕnēlēḵāh wĕʾēlĕḵāh lĕnegdĕḵā — "Let us journey on, and I will go alongside you." Esau's proposal to accompany Jacob — lĕnegdĕḵā (before/alongside you) — is the gesture of a protective escort. Whether Esau's offer is magnanimous or carries a latent territorial dimension (escorting Jacob through his domain) is ambiguous. Jacob's response in vv13-14 declines politely; the subsequent action (v17) reveals that Jacob goes not toward Seir as promised, but to Succoth in the opposite direction.
13: hayyĕlāḏîm rakkîm wĕhаṣṣōʾn wĕhabbāqār ʿālôt ʿālāy — "The children are tender (rakkîm) and the nursing ewes and cows are a concern to me." Rakkîm (plural of rakh, H7390, tender, soft, delicate) — the same adjective applied to Leah's eyes in 29:17. Jacob uses the vulnerability of his children and animals as his pretext for travelling separately. The concern for the nursing animals (meyniqqot) reflects authentic pastoral knowledge — overdriving nursing stock for even one day risks their deaths.
14: yaʿăḇōr-nāʾ ʾăḏōnî lipnê ʿaḇdô wĕʾănî ʾetnahălāh lĕʾiṭṭî lĕregeḡ hammĕlāʾḵāh ʾăšer-lĕpānay — "Let my lord go ahead while I move slowly, at the pace of the work (lĕregeḡ hamĕlāʾḵāh) before me." Regeḡ (H7272, the foot/pace) — a late and rare term for "at the pace/step of"; the phrase lĕregeḡ hamĕlāʾḵāh means "at the step-rate of the livestock." The courtly address (ʾăḏōnî, my lord; ʿaḇdô, his servant) is sustained throughout — Jacob does not relax his deferential register even while arranging the separation. His stated destination in Seir is a narrative fiction; he goes to Succoth (v17).
15: wayyōʾmer ʿēśāw ʾaṣṣîḡāh-nāʾ ʿimmĕḵā minh-āʿām ʾăšer ʾittî wayyōʾmer lāmmāh zeh ʾemṣāʾ-ḥēn bĕʿênê ʾăḏōnî — "'Let me leave some of my men with you,' said Esau. 'Why should that be? Let me only find favor in my lord's eyes.'" Jacob's deflection — "why should that be?" — is gracious but firm. His use of māṣāʾ ḥēn bĕʿênê ʾăḏōnî (find favor in my lord's eyes) echoes the covenant-favor language of 6:8 (Noah found favor), 30:27 (Laban found favor through Jacob), and 32:5 (Jacob's own message to Esau). Jacob keeps his freedom of movement while closing the encounter with honor.
16: wayyāšāḇ bayyôm hahûʾ ʿēśāw lĕḏarkô śēʿîrāh — "So that day Esau returned on his way to Seir." The narrative's economy is stark: the anticipated armed encounter (32:7-8) resolves in a single chapter; Esau departs the narrative quietly. Jacob promised to come to Esau at Seir (v14); the narrative shows him going to Succoth instead (v17). Whether this is deliberate evasion or a planned delay that is never fulfilled, the narrator does not comment. The silence about Jacob's Seir-promise creates a moral ambiguity that the text neither resolves nor ignores.
17: wĕsukkōt ʿāśāh lô ḇāyit ûlĕmiqnēhû ʿāśāh sukkōt ʿal-kēn qārāʾ šēm-hammāqôm sukkôt — "He built a house for himself and made booths for his livestock; therefore the place is called Succoth." Sukkôt (H5523) — the toponym derived from sukkōt (H5521, booths/shelters, plural of sukkāh). The Feast of Booths (Ḥag hasukkôt) will commemorate Israel's wilderness wandering in booths (Lev 23:42-43); the settlement at Succoth in the Jordan valley (modern Tell Deir ʿAlla) is the first named settlement of Jacob's household in Canaan proper, and it is named for the agricultural/pastoral character of his construction rather than for a theophany — unlike virtually every other patriarchal place-name.
18: wayyāḇōʾ yaʿăqōḇ šālēm ʿîr šĕḵem ʾăšer bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan — "Jacob came safely (šālēm) to the city of Shechem." The word šālēm (H8003, complete, whole, unharmed) is a deliberate word-choice: it echoes šālôm (covenant peace/wholeness) and may also function as a proper noun — the ancient city name Shalem (cf. Ps 76:2, Salem = Jerusalem, city of Melchizedek). Jacob's arrival "in peace/wholeness" fulfills the vow condition of 28:21 ("if I return safely to my father's house"). The city of Shechem is the site of the first covenant meal with Canaanites (v20) — and the site of the violence that will follow in ch 34.
19: wayyiqen ʾet-ḥelqat haśśāḏeh ʾăšer nāṭāh-šām ʾohălô miyyaḏ bĕnê-ḥămôr ʾăḇî šĕḵem bĕmēʾāh qĕśîṭāh — "He bought the plot of ground where he pitched his tent from the sons of Hamor for a hundred qĕśîṭāh." Qĕśîṭāh (H7192) — a monetary unit of uncertain weight or composition, occurring only here, in Josh 24:32 (for the same Shechem plot, confirmed as Joseph's burial place), and in Job 42:11. The purchase legally grounds Jacob's household in Canaan — not merely as a sojourner camping with permission, but as a landowner with a purchased title. This plot will eventually contain Joseph's bones (Exod 13:19; Josh 24:32; Acts 7:16).
20: wayyaṣṣeḇ-šām mizḇēaḥ wayyiqrāʾ-lô ʾēl ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — "He erected an altar there and called it El Elohe Israel — God, the God of Israel." The altar-name ʾēl ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl is a declaration: the God of the covenant is identified as the God of the man now named Israel. Jacob does not yet build the Bethel altar (that is commanded in 35:1); the Shechem altar is a spontaneous act of worship. The use of his new covenant name (Israel) in the altar's name is the first recorded instance of Jacob publicly using the Peniel identity. The naming pattern — "God is the God of Israel" — will become the covenant formula repeated throughout the OT (Exod 5:1; Josh 7:13; 2 Kgs 18:31).
